'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were other_designation1 {{ill|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:11]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動}}

But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ancient_Kofun_in_Japan |name=Tsukinowa Kofun |location={{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Kume District, Okayama|ja|久米郡}}{{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町}} Iioka, Shuma-no-oku {{Japan location|34|56|30.66|134|6|32.06|Misaki, Iioka|34.94185,134.108906|Tsukinowa Kofun|nocoord=yes}} |coordinates={{Coord|34|56|30.66|N|134|6|32.06|E|}} |type={{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |map_size=Diameter 60m, Height 10m |built=Early 5th century |burial={{ill|Wooden coffin|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}{{ill|Clay chamber|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} (2 units) |Buried_Person=Unknown |burial goods=Necklaces, mirrors, {{ill|Tankō|ja|短甲|1=Tankō}}, swords, combs, iron needles, etc. |other_designation={{ill|Okayama Prefecture Designated other_designation|ja|岡山県指定文化財一覧#史跡}}, other_designation1 in 1959 |image=[[File:Tsukinowa Kofun 01.JPG|250px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=250}} |map=Japan Okayama }}
'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were other_designation1 {{ill|[[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:11]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動}}

So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ancient_Kofun_in_Japan |name=Tsukinowa Kofun |location={{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Kume District, Okayama|ja|久米郡}}{{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町}} Iioka, Shuma-no-oku {{Japan location|34|56|30.66|134|6|32.06|Misaki, Iioka|34.94185,134.108906|Tsukinowa Kofun|nocoord=yes}} |coordinates={{Coord|34|56|30.66|N|134|6|32.06|E|}} |type={{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |map_size=Diameter 60m, Height 10m |built=Early 5th century |burial={{ill|Wooden coffin|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}{{ill|Clay chamber|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} (2 units) |Buried_Person=Unknown |burial goods=Necklaces, mirrors, {{ill|Tankō|ja|短甲|1=Tankō}}, swords, combs, iron needles, etc. |other_designation={{ill|Okayama Prefecture Designated other_designation|ja|岡山県指定文化財一覧#史跡}}, other_designation1 in 1959 |image=[[File:Tsukinowa Kofun 01.JPG|250px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=250}} |map=Japan Okayama }}
'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were other_designation1 {{ill|[[Important designation1 (Japan)|Important designation1]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:11]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動}}

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were other_designation1 {{ill|[[Important designation1 (Japan)|Important designation1]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:11]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動}}

But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ancient_Kofun_in_Japan |name=Tsukinowa Kofun |location={{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Kume District, Okayama|ja|久米郡}}{{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町}} Iioka, Shuma-no-oku {{Japan location|34|56|30.66|134|6|32.06|Misaki, Iioka|34.94185,134.108906|Tsukinowa Kofun|nocoord=yes}} |coordinates={{Coord|34|56|30.66|N|134|6|32.06|E|}} |type={{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |map_size=Diameter 60m, Height 10m |built=Early 5th century |burial={{ill|Wooden coffin|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}{{ill|Clay chamber|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} (2 units) |Interred_individual=Unknown |burial goods=Necklaces, mirrors, {{ill|Tankō|ja|短甲|1=Tankō}}, swords, combs, iron needles, etc. |other_designation={{ill|Okayama Prefecture Designated other_designation|ja|岡山県指定文化財一覧#史跡}}, other_designation1 in 1959 |image=[[File:Tsukinowa Kofun 01.JPG|250px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=250}} |map=Japan Okayama }}
'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were other_designation1 {{ill|[[Important designation1 (Japan)|Important designation1]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:11]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動}}

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 {{ill|[[Important designation1 (Japan)|Important designation1]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ancient_Kofun_in_Japan |name=Tsukinowa Kofun |location={{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Kume District, Okayama|ja|久米郡}}{{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町}} Iioka, Shuma-no-oku {{Japan location|34|56|30.66|134|6|32.06|Misaki, Iioka|34.94185,134.108906|Tsukinowa Kofun|nocoord=yes}} |coordinates={{Coord|34|56|30.66|N|134|6|32.06|E|}} |type={{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |map_size=Diameter 60m, Height 10m |built=Early 5th century |burial={{ill|Wooden coffin|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}{{ill|Clay chamber|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} (2 units) |Interred_individual=Unknown |burial goods=Necklaces, mirrors, {{ill|Tankō|ja|短甲|1=Tankō}}, swords, combs, iron needles, etc. |Historic Site={{ill|Okayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site|ja|岡山県指定文化財一覧#史跡}}, Historic Site1 in 1959 |image=[[File:Tsukinowa Kofun 01.JPG|250px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=250}} |map=Japan Okayama }}
'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 {{ill|[[Important designation1 (Japan)|Important designation1]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:11]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動}}

Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ancient_Kofun_in_Japan |name=Tsukinowa Kofun |location={{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Kume District, Okayama|ja|久米郡}}{{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町}} Iioka, Shuma-no-oku {{Japan location|34|56|30.66|134|6|32.06|Misaki, Iioka|34.94185,134.108906|Tsukinowa Kofun|nocoord=yes}} |coordinates={{Coord|34|56|30.66|N|134|6|32.06|E|}} |type={{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} |map_size=Diameter 60m, Height 10m |built=Early 5th century |burial={{ill|Wooden coffin|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}{{ill|Clay chamber|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber}} (2 units) |Interred_individual=Unknown |burial goods=Necklaces, mirrors, {{ill|Tankō|ja|短甲|1=Tankō}}, swords, combs, iron needles, etc. |Historic Site={{ill|Okayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site|ja|岡山県指定文化財一覧#史跡}}, Historic Site1 in 1959 |image=[[File:Tsukinowa Kofun 01.JPG|250px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=250}} |map=Japan Okayama }}
'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 {{ill|[[Important designation1 (Japan)|Important designation1]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.okayama.jp/kyoiku/bunka/hogo/bunkazai/sitei/ken_ichiran.html#k_kinen_shiseki Okayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site] ]] <!--Tsukinowa Kofun--><!--Tsukinowa Kofun-->

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町|12=draft}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=draft}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=draft}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=draft}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学|12=draft}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=draft}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=draft}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 {{ill|[[Important designation1 (Japan)|Important designation1]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆|12=draft}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|12=draft}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動|12=draft}}

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町|12=draft}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=draft}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=draft}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=draft}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学|12=draft}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=draft}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=draft}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 {{ill|[[Important designation1 (Japan)|Important designation1]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆|12=draft}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|12=draft}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動|12=draft}}

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町|12=draft}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=draft}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=draft}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=draft}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学|12=draft}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=draft}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=draft}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 {{ill|[[Important designation1 (Japan)|Important designation1]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆|12=draft}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|12=draft}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動|12=draft}}

This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ancient_Kofun_in_Japan |name=Tsukinowa Kofun |location={{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}{{ill|Kume District, Okayama|ja|久米郡|12=draft}}{{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町|12=draft}} Iioka, Shuma-no-oku {{Japan location|34|56|30.66|134|6|32.06|Misaki, Iioka|34.94185,134.108906|Tsukinowa Kofun|nocoord=yes}} |coordinates={{Coord|34|56|30.66|N|134|6|32.06|E|}} |type={{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft}} |map_size=Diameter 60m, Height 10m |built=Early 5th century |burial={{ill|Wooden coffin|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}{{ill|Clay chamber|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=draft}} (2 units) |Interred_individual=Unknown |burial goods=Necklaces, mirrors, {{ill|Tankō|ja|短甲|1=Tankō|12=draft}}, swords, combs, iron needles, etc. |Historic Site={{ill|Okayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site|ja|岡山県指定文化財一覧#史跡|12=draft}}, Historic Site1 in 1959 |image=[[File:Tsukinowa Kofun 01.JPG|250px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=250}} |map=Japan Okayama }}
'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町|12=draft}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=draft}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=draft}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=draft}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学|12=draft}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=draft}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=draft}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 {{ill|[[Important designation1 (Japan)|Important designation1]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆|12=draft}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|12=draft}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.okayama.jp/kyoiku/bunka/hogo/bunkazai/sitei/ken_ichiran.html#k_kinen_shiseki Okayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site] ]] <!--Tsukinowa Kofun--><!--Tsukinowa Kofun-->

This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ancient_Kofun_in_Japan |name=Tsukinowa Kofun |location={{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}{{ill|Kume District, Okayama|ja|久米郡|12=draft}}{{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町|12=draft}} Iioka, Shuma-no-oku {{Japan location|34|56|30.66|134|6|32.06|Misaki, Iioka|34.94185,134.108906|Tsukinowa Kofun|nocoord=yes}} |coordinates={{Coord|34|56|30.66|N|134|6|32.06|E|}} |type={{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft}} |map_size=Diameter 60m, Height 10m |built=Early 5th century |burial={{ill|Wooden coffin|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}{{ill|Clay chamber|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=draft}} (2 units) |Interred_individual=Unknown |burial goods=Necklaces, mirrors, {{ill|Tankō|ja|短甲|1=Tankō|12=draft}}, swords, combs, iron needles, etc. |Historic Site={{ill|Okayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site|ja|岡山県指定文化財一覧#史跡|12=draft}}, Historic Site1 in 1959 |image=[[File:Tsukinowa Kofun 01.JPG|250px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=250}} |map=Japan Okayama }}
'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町|12=draft}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=draft}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=draft}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=draft}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学|12=draft}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=draft}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=draft}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 {{ill|[[Important designation1 (Japan)|Important designation1]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆|12=draft}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|12=draft}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.okayama.jp/kyoiku/bunka/hogo/bunkazai/sitei/ken_ichiran.html#k_kinen_shiseki Okayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site] ]] <!--Tsukinowa Kofun--><!--Tsukinowa Kofun-->

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町|12=draft}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=draft}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=draft}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=draft}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学|12=draft}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=draft}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=draft}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 {{ill|[[Important designation1 (Japan)|Important designation1]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆|12=draft}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|12=draft}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動|12=draft}}

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町|12=draft}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=draft}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=draft}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=draft}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学|12=draft}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=draft}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=draft}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 {{ill|[[Important designation1 (Japan)|Important designation1]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆|12=draft}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|12=draft}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.okayama.jp/kyoiku/bunka/hogo/bunkazai/sitei/ken_ichiran.html#k_kinen_shiseki Okayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site] ]] <!--Tsukinowa Kofun--><!--Tsukinowa Kofun-->

Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|12=draft}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町|12=draft}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=draft}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=draft}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=draft}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学|12=draft}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=draft}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=draft}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 {{ill|[[Important designation1 (Japan)|Important designation1]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆|12=draft}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|12=draft}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動|12=draft}}
