Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆|12=draft}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|12=draft}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動|12=draft}}

{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Ancient_Kofun_in_Japan |name=Tsukinowa Kofun |location={{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}{{ill|Kume District, Okayama|ja|久米郡|12=draft}}{{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町|12=draft}} Iioka, Shuma-no-oku {{Japan location|34|56|30.66|134|6|32.06|Misaki, Iioka|34.94185,134.108906|Tsukinowa Kofun|nocoord=yes}} |coordinates={{Coord|34|56|30.66|N|134|6|32.06|E|}} |type={{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft}} |map_size=Diameter 60m, Height 10m |built=Early 5th century |burial={{ill|Wooden coffin|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}{{ill|Clay chamber|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=draft}} (2 units) |Interred_individual=Unknown |burial goods=Necklaces, mirrors, {{ill|Tankō|ja|短甲|1=Tankō|12=draft}}, swords, combs, iron needles, etc. |Historic Site={{ill|Okayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site|ja|岡山県指定文化財一覧#史跡|12=draft}}, Historic Site1 in 1959 |image=[[File:Tsukinowa Kofun 01.JPG|250px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=250}} |map=Japan Okayama }}
'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|ja|美咲町|12=draft}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}.
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=draft}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=draft}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=draft}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学|12=draft}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=draft}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=draft}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 {{ill|{{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|ko|일본의_중요문화재}} of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆|12=draft}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|12=draft}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動|12=draft}}

This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=draft}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|12=draft}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=draft}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|fukiishi|ja|葺石|12=draft}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=draft}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=draft}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|ja|岡山大学|12=draft}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=draft}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=draft}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 {{ill|{{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|ko|일본의_중요문화재}} of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon festival|ja|お盆|12=draft}}.
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|12=draft}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.okayama.jp/kyoiku/bunka/hogo/bunkazai/sitei/ken_ichiran.html#k_kinen_shiseki Okayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site] ]] <!--Tsukinowa Kofun--><!--Tsukinowa Kofun-->

revision.text:
{{日本の古墳 |名称=月の輪古墳 |所在地=[[岡山県]][[久米郡]][[美咲町]]飯岡字主馬ノ奥{{日本の位置情報|34|56|30.66|134|6|32.06|美咲町飯岡|34.94185,134.108906|月の輪古墳|nocoord=yes}} |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|56|30.66|N|134|6|32.06|E|}} |形状=[[円墳]] |規模=直径60[[メートル|m]]、高さ10m |築造年代=5世紀前半 |埋葬施設=[[木棺]][[粘土槨]]2基 |被葬者=不明 |出土品=首飾り、鏡、[[短甲]]、刀、櫛、鉄針など |史跡指定=[[昭和]]34年（[[1959年]]）[[岡山県指定文化財一覧#史跡|県指定]] |画像=[[Image:Tsukinowa Kofun 01.JPG|250px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=250}} |地図=Japan Okayama }}
'''月の輪古墳'''（つきのわこふん）は、[[岡山県]][[美咲町]]飯岡（ゆうか）の大平山（標高320[[メートル]]）山頂に[[5世紀]]前半に築かれた[[古墳]]である。
== 概要 == 本古墳は、年代からも、内容からも、地域集団の首長の墳墓であると考えられている<ref>近藤(2001)260ページ</ref>。 直径60メートル、高さ10メートルの大型[[円墳]]で中腹に1メートルの段が設けられ、北側裾に[[造り出し]]がある。墳頂の平坦部は直径17メートルで、[[木棺]][[粘土槨]]が2基あった。斜面には角ばった石で[[葺石]]が敷かれ、墳頂周囲・段上・裾には[[円筒埴輪]]と[[朝顔形埴輪]]が並べられていた。
[[1953年]]（[[昭和]]28年）8月から12月にかけて、[[岡山大学]]助手（当時）の[[近藤義郎]]を中心に地元の住民や教師、学生らによって[[発掘調査]]が行われた。この調査は、周辺地域の関連遺跡と月の輪古墳の全面発掘が行われた。こうして[[弥生時代]]以降の生産力の高まり、さらに前期古墳の歴史的性格を理論的実証的に明らかにする道を開いた<ref>近藤(2001)482ページ</ref>。この一連の活動・調査は「月の輪方式」と呼ばれ学術的、教育的、また地域運動としても当時の話題となった。この記録は翌[[1954年]]（昭和29年）[[スライド]]と記録[[映画]]となり、文集『月の輪教室』として記録された。古墳は[[1959年]]（昭和34年）[[9月15日]]に[[岡山県指定文化財一覧#史跡|岡山県指定史跡]]に指定された。
この調査で墳頂部の2基の木棺のうち、中央のものは全長565[[センチメートル]]で[[男性]]と見られる遺骸と[[首飾り]]・[[鏡]]・鉄製短甲・革製草摺・[[刀]]・[[剣]]・[[鉄鏃]]・銅鏃などの[[副葬品]]が出土した。南側の[[木棺]]は全長310センチメートルで[[女性]]と見られる遺骸と首飾り・[[漆]]塗[[櫛|竹櫛]]・鏡・刀・剣・鉄[[針]]・[[石釧]]などの副葬品が出土した。北側裾の造り出しからも木棺粘土槨が発見されたが中からは何も出土しなかった。
これらの出土品は、山麓の飯岡地区に[[1974年]]（昭和49年）建造された文化財収蔵庫（月の輪郷土資料館）に収蔵されており、[[1984年]]（昭和59年）[[4月10日]]に[[岡山県指定文化財一覧#考古資料|岡山県指定重要文化財]]に指定された。飯岡地区では毎年[[8月15日]]頃「月の輪踊りの夕べ」を開催し、[[お盆|盆祭り]]のなかで月の輪古墳発掘調査を記念している。
== 所在地 == *岡山県久米郡美咲町飯岡
== 月の輪地域 == 岡山県の中東部、[[吉井川]]・吉野川の合流地点付近を中心とする地域を、月の輪地域と呼ぶこととする。この地域一帯は、弥生時代中期なかば過ぎると開拓も進み、人々が定着するところも広がった。[[扇状地]]や標高約300メートルの[[吉備高原]]などに弥生時代の遺物発見地がおよそ50余カ所ある。後期には、[[沖積平野]]の一部特に低湿地帯の水田化が始まったと考えられている。月の輪古墳の位置する地域は飯岡上（ゆうかかみ）地区で、[[山塊]]や[[丘陵]]および扇状地である。[[古墳時代]]の集落遺跡は、[[土師器]]や[[須恵器]]の包含層によって知られ、後期には、集落の分布が流域の平地や付近の山裾に拡大している。このことは古墳時代には沖積平野の水田化が進んだことを示しており、平地の開墾という事業が進められ、人々の集団を結合させる役割を担ったと考えられる。そして、集団の結合が首長墳として現れる。首長墳と思われる月の輪古墳を含む地域に古墳が集中しており、22基知られている。そのうち15基は後期古墳に推定される小墳、2基は時期不明で[[箱式石棺]]をもつ小墳、5基は、1基ははっきりしないが4基は前期と考えられ、比較的大型で、山頂や[[尾根]]上に位置している。そのうち、特に月の輪古墳と釜の上古墳（円墳径55メートル、埴輪・葺石あり、おそらく木棺粘土槨）が大きい。<ref>近藤(2001)226-236ページ</ref>
== 脚注 == <references />
== 参考文献 == *岡山県高等学校教育研究会社会科部会歴史分科会/編 『新版 岡山県の歴史散歩』 [[山川出版社]] 1991年 187ページ *近藤義郎「月の輪古墳の発掘」/近藤義郎『日本考古学研究序説』[[岩波書店]] 2001年 ISBN 4-00-001769-1
== 関連項目 == {{Commons|Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} *[[日本の古墳一覧]] *[[国民的歴史学運動]]
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.pref.okayama.jp/kyoiku/bunka/hogo/bunkazai/sitei/ken_ichiran.html#k_kinen_shiseki 岡山県指定文化財]
{{デフォルトソート:つきのわこふん}} [[category:岡山県の古墳]] [[Category:岡山県指定史跡]] [[Category:岡山県指定重要文化財]] [[Category:美咲町の歴史]] [[Category:円墳]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Tsukinowa Kofun]][[ja:月の輪古墳]]
{{Infobox ancient site|location={{ill|Kume district|qid=Q1131568}}; {{ill|Misaki|qid=Q1156100}}; {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|qid=Q132936}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.94185|N|134.10890555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|circular kofun|qid=Q11394747}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11515782]]
Wikidata name: Tsukinowa Kofun
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in [[:en:Misaki, Okayama|Misaki, Okayama]], Iioka district, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]].

== Overview ==

This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Platform-style projection|lt=Platform-style projection|lt=Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuridashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 造出}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|1=Wooden Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}. The mound was covered with angular [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning-glory haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.

In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at [[:en:Okayama University|Okayama University]], with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.

The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kusazuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone bracelets|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.

The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local [[:en:Obon|Obon festival]].

== location ==
* Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan

== Tsukinowa Region ==
The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>

Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.

The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kusazuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone bracelets|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.

The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local [[:en:Obon|Obon festival]].

== location ==
* Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan

== Tsukinowa Region ==
The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|qq=|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in [[:en:Misaki, Okayama|Misaki, Okayama]], Iioka district, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]].

== Overview ==

This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Platform-style projection|lt=Platform-style projection|lt=Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuridashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 造出}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|1=Wooden Coffin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}. The mound was covered with angular [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning-glory haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.

In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at [[:en:Okayama University|Okayama University]], with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.

The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kusazuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone bracelets|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.

The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local [[:en:Obon|Obon festival]].

== location ==
* Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan

== Tsukinowa Region ==
The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in [[:en:Misaki, Okayama|Misaki, Okayama]], Iioka district, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]].

== Overview ==

This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Platform-style projection|lt=Platform-style projection|lt=Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuridashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 造出}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|1=Wooden Coffin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}. The mound was covered with angular [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning-glory haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.

In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at [[:en:Okayama University|Okayama University]], with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.

The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kusazuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone bracelets|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.

The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local [[:en:Obon|Obon festival]].

== location ==
* Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan

== Tsukinowa Region ==
The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>

These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in [[:en:Misaki, Okayama|Misaki, Okayama]], Iioka district, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]].

== Overview ==

This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Platform-style projection|lt=Platform-style projection|lt=Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuridashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 造出}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|1=Wooden Coffin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}. The mound was covered with angular [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning-glory haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.

In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at [[:en:Okayama University|Okayama University]], with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.

The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kusazuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone bracelets|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.

The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local [[:en:Obon|Obon festival]].

== location ==
* Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan

== Tsukinowa Region ==
The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in [[:en:Misaki, Okayama|Misaki, Okayama]], Iioka district, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]].

== Overview ==

This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Platform-style projection|lt=Platform-style projection|lt=Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuridashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 造出}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|1=Wooden Coffin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}. The mound was covered with angular [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning-glory haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.

In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at [[:en:Okayama University|Okayama University]], with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.

The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kusazuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone bracelets|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.

The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local [[:en:Obon|Obon festival]].

== location ==
* Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan

== Tsukinowa Region ==
The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>

So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. '''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in [[:en:Misaki, Okayama|Misaki, Okayama]], Iioka district, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]].
== Overview ==
This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Platform-style projection|lt=Platform-style projection|lt=Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuridashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 造出}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|1=Wooden Coffin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}. The mound was covered with angular [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning-glory haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.
In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at [[:en:Okayama University|Okayama University]], with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.
The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kusazuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone bracelets|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.
The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local [[:en:Obon|Obon festival]].
== location == * Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan
== Tsukinowa Region == The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187. * Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1. == See Also == {{Commons|Category:kk]][[Category:Tsukinowa Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|Infobox ancient site一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|National History Movement|ja|国民的歴史学運動|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National History Movement|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.okayama.jp/kyoiku/bunka/hogo/bunkazai/sitei/ken_ichiran.html#k_kinen_shiseki Okayama Prefecture Designated Historic Site] ]]

== Overview ==

This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Platform-style projection|lt=Platform-style projection|lt=Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuridashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 造出}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|1=Wooden Coffin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}. The mound was covered with angular [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning-glory haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.

In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at [[:en:Okayama University|Okayama University]], with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.

The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kusazuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone bracelets|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.

The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local [[:en:Obon|Obon festival]].

== location ==
* Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan

== Tsukinowa Region ==
The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in [[:en:Misaki, Okayama|Misaki, Okayama]], Iioka district, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]].

== Overview ==

This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Platform-style projection|lt=Platform-style projection|lt=Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuridashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 造出}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|1=Wooden Coffin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}. The mound was covered with angular [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning-glory haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.

In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at [[:en:Okayama University|Okayama University]], with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.

The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kusazuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone bracelets|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.

The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local [[:en:Obon|Obon festival]].

== location ==
* Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan

== Tsukinowa Region ==
The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>

In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at [[:en:Okayama University|Okayama University]], with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.

The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kusazuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone bracelets|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.

The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local [[:en:Obon|Obon festival]].

== location ==
* Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan

== Tsukinowa Region ==
The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in [[:en:Misaki, Okayama|Misaki, Okayama]], Iioka district, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]].

== Overview ==

This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Platform-style projection|lt=Platform-style projection|lt=Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuridashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 造出}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|1=Wooden Coffin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}. The mound was covered with angular [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning-glory haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.

In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at [[:en:Okayama University|Okayama University]], with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.

The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kusazuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone bracelets|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.

The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local [[:en:Obon|Obon festival]].

== location ==
* Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan

== Tsukinowa Region ==
The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in [[:en:Misaki, Okayama|Misaki, Okayama]], Iioka district, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]].

== Overview ==

This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Platform-style projection|lt=Platform-style projection|lt=Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuridashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 造出}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|1=Wooden Coffin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}. The mound was covered with angular [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning-glory haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.

In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at [[:en:Okayama University|Okayama University]], with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.

The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kusazuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone bracelets|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.

The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local [[:en:Obon|Obon festival]].

== location ==
* Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan

== Tsukinowa Region ==
The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>

<references />

== Further Reading ==
* Okayama Prefectural High School Education Research Society History Division, *New Edition: History Walk of Okayama Prefecture*. Yamato Publishing, 1991, p. 187.
* Yoshirō Kondō, *Introduction to Japanese Archaeological Studies*, Iwanami Shoten, 2001. ISBN 4-00-001769-1.

In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at [[:en:Okayama University|Okayama University]], with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.

The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kusazuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone bracelets|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.

The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local [[:en:Obon|Obon festival]].

== location ==
* Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan

== Tsukinowa Region ==
The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>

'''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in [[:en:Misaki, Okayama|Misaki, Okayama]], Iioka district, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]].

== Overview ==

This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Platform-style projection|lt=Platform-style projection|lt=Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuridashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 造出}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|1=Wooden Coffin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}. The mound was covered with angular [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning-glory haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.

In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at [[:en:Okayama University|Okayama University]], with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.

The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kusazuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone bracelets|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.

The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local [[:en:Obon|Obon festival]].

== location ==
* Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan

== Tsukinowa Region ==
The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. '''Tsukinowa Kofun''' (月の輪古墳) is an ancient {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}} constructed in the early 5th century. It is located atop Ōhirayama (elevation 320 meters) in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|en|Misaki, Okayama|WD=Q1156100|lt=Misaki, Okayama}}, Iioka district, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}.

== Overview ==

This kofun is believed to be the tomb of a regional chief based on its map_size and archaeological findings.<ref>Yoshirō Kondō (2001), p. 260</ref> Measuring 60 meters in diameter and 10 meters in height, it features a terrace approximately 1 meter wide at the midsection, and a {{ill|Platform-style projection|lt=Platform-style projection|lt=Tsuridashi|ja|造り出し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuridashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 造出}} on its northern side. The flat top, 17 meters in diameter, housed two {{ill|wooden coffins|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|1=Wooden Coffin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Wooden Coffin|qq=}}{{ill|clay chambers|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay chambers|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}. The mound was covered with angular {{ill|Fukiishi|en|Fukiishi|WD=Q11621213|lt=fukiishi}} stones, and {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} and {{ill|morning-glory haniwa|ja|朝顔形埴輪|1=Morning glory-shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/morning-glory haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Morning glory-shaped haniwa|qq=}} were arranged around the summit and terrace.

In 1953 (Shōwa 28), an excavation was conducted from August to December under the supervision of Yoshirō Kondō, then an assistant at {{ill|Okayama University|en|Okayama University|WD=Q1067916|lt=Okayama University}}, with participation from local residents, teachers, and students. The excavation also included related archaeological sites in the vicinity. This comprehensive research paved the way for understanding the economic developments of the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period}} and the historical characteristics of early kofun.<ref>Kondō (2001), p. 482</ref> This project, known as the "Tsukinowa Method," gained attention for its academic, educational, and community-driven approach. Documentation of the excavation was preserved in slides, a film, and the anthology *Tsukinowa Classroom* in 1954.

The main burial site on the summit contained two wooden coffins. The central coffin (length: 565 cm) held the remains of a presumed male, along with grave goods such as necklaces, mirrors, iron armor, leather {{ill|kusazuri|ja|草摺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kusazuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, swords, iron and bronze arrowheads, and daggers. The southern coffin (length: 310 cm) contained the remains of a presumed female, with burial goods such as lacquered combs, mirrors, iron needles, and {{ill|stone bracelets|ja|石釧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone bracelets|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. A third wooden chamber on the northern terrace contained no burial goods.

The burial goods are preserved at the Tsukinowa Local Museum, constructed in 1974 in the Iioka district. The items were Historic Site1 {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft}} on April 10, 1984. Each year in mid-August, the "Tsukinowa Dance Evening" commemorates the excavation as part of the local {{ill|Obon|en|Obon|WD=Q1154888|lt=Obon festival}}.

== location ==
* Misaki Town, Iioka District, Okayama Prefecture, Japan

== Tsukinowa Region ==
The Tsukinowa area includes the confluence of the Yoshii and Yoshino Rivers in central-eastern Okayama Prefecture. This region saw significant settlement during the middle {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period}}, evidenced by over 50 archaeological sites. By the late Yayoi period, wet rice farming likely developed in the alluvial plains, with settlements expanding into the surrounding foothills. Tsukinowa Kofun is located in a prominent area, part of a cluster of 22 Kofuns. These include 15 small mounds from the late kofun period, two uncertain-period mounds with box-style {{ill|Sekkan|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, and five larger early kofun mounds positioned on ridges or peaks. The Tsukinowa and Kama-no-ue Kofun (diameter: 55 meters, with haniwa and fukiishi) stand out in size.<ref>Kondō (2001), pp. 226–236</ref>
